text: The robotic right inguinal hernia repair is performed using the transabdominal preperitoneal approach. The patient is positioned in a supine lithotomy position, facilitating access to the surgical site.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a trocar setup similar to laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair, using two 8mm trocars in the right and left mid quadrants, centered by a 12mm trocar for the camera. The robotic system is docked between the patient's legs.

The internal surface anatomy is visible, showing the medial umbilical ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, spermatic vessels, and vas deferens. A pantaloon-type hernia with both direct and indirect components is observed.

The surgeon begins the dissection by creating a peritoneal flap, starting medially at the medial umbilical ligament and extending laterally towards the anterior superior iliac spine. The dissection is performed dorsal to the transversalis fascia to remain within an avascular plane.
The surgical view shows the iliotibial tract marked by an arrow. The genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible, and Cooper's ligament is exposed medial to the hernia sac.
The surgeon exposes the coronamortis overlying Cooper's ligament to prevent inadvertent TAC injury. With Cooper's ligament clearly visible, the dissection of the hernia sac begins.

The surgeon reduces the hernia sac from the deep inguinal ring while skeletonizing the spermatic cord using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The testicular vessels are visible to the right of the scissors and are carefully preserved along their length.

The surgeon uses scissors to delineate the edge of the reduced peritoneal sac, continuing the dissection of the peritoneum from its retroperitoneal structures to prepare for the placement of a wide sheet of mesh.

The surgeon places tacks to secure the mesh medially on Cooper's ligament and laterally above the iliopubic tract, avoiding the triangle of doom and the triangle of pain. The assistant deploys the mesh through an eight millimeter trocar into the inguinal space, after undocking it from the ipsilateral side of the hernia defect.
The surgeon places tacks on Cooper's ligament, above it anteriorly, and in the supralateral position. Subsequently, the peritoneal flap is closed using the attacking device.
